origin: Temperate Eurasia — mountain woodlands from Europe east to Mongolia
habitat: Woodland edges, clearings, and subalpine meadows on humus-rich, often limy soils
blurb: The aristocrat of the shade border and the parent of the martagon hybrids: a tall, slow-building lily that throws a single straight stem hung with up to thirty small nodding flowers, each with petals curled fully back into the unmistakable 'Turk's cap' — rosy-pink to white, freckled inside with dark maroon, and lightly fragrant. The lance-shaped leaves sit in tidy whorls up the stem. Unlike the gaudy Asiatic and Oriental lilies, the martagon is a woodlander: it wants part shade, moist humus-rich soil, and patience — it sulks the first year or two and resents being moved — but once settled it is long-lived, all but pest-free, and slowly forms a colony. Its tolerance of slightly alkaline, clayey ground makes it one of the better true lilies for the Front Range. (Like all true lilies it is deadly to cats.)
sun: Part shade to full sun; afternoon shade is ideal in our intense high-altitude sun (more shade gives taller stems)
water: Moderate — keep the soil evenly moist through the growing season; not a xeric or drought-tolerant plant, but needs sharp winter drainage
spread: Slow-clumping from a bulb; settled plants form small colonies over years and resent being dug or moved
seasons: Whorled leafy shoots push up in spring; a single stem of nodding Turk's-cap flowers opens mid-to-late summer; foliage yellows and the stem is cut back in fall as the plant dies down to a dormant bulb — no winter presence above ground
wildlife: The fragrant, nectar-rich nodding flowers are worked by long-tongued bees, hawkmoths, and butterflies (notably swallowtails), and draw hummingbirds
deer: Not deer-resistant — deer browse the buds and flowers, and rabbits and voles dig and eat the shoots and bulbs
toxic: All parts are highly toxic to cats — ingesting even a small amount (or grooming off pollen, or drinking vase water) causes acute kidney failure and death; there is no safe amount for a cat. Non-toxic to dogs and horses. The ornamental bulbs are not a food and should not be eaten by people

care.hardiness: Reliably hardy in USDA zones 3–8 (some sources extend it to 9), so the martagon lily is fully winter-hardy across the entire Front Range (Boulder is zone 5b–6a) and needs no winter protection in the ground.[1][3] It is one of the longest-lived lilies — established plants persist and slowly spread for decades — but it is slow to settle and may not flower at all its first year after planting.[1]
care.planting: Plant the dormant bulbs in fall (September–October) so the roots establish before hard freeze; martagon bulbs have no papery tunic and dry out quickly, so plant them promptly and never let them sit out. Set them roughly three times the bulb's height deep (about 4–6 in.) in humus-rich, well-drained soil, ideally where they get afternoon shade.[3][1] Choose the spot carefully and leave it: martagons resent disturbance and should not be moved once established, and they often skip bloom the first season while they settle.[1] In containers they grow but the bulbs are far less cold-hardy than in the ground — sink the pot or overwinter it in an unheated garage so it doesn't freeze out.[3]
care.sun: Part shade is ideal — the martagon is a woodland-edge plant, and a spot with morning sun and afternoon shade (or dappled light) gives the best, longest-lasting bloom in our hot, high-altitude summers; the more shade it gets, the taller the flowering stem grows. It will take full sun in cooler, moister situations but scorches and stresses in full afternoon sun here.[1]
care.soil: Best in moist, slightly alkaline, organically rich, well-draining sandy loam — and, unusually for a lily, it tolerates some clay and lime, which makes it well suited to Boulder's alkaline soils.[1] Lighten heavy clay with compost to improve drainage, and top-dress the planting site with leaf mold or compost each fall to feed the surface roots and mimic its woodland-floor home. The one thing it will not abide is waterlogged ground, which rots the bulb.[1]
care.water: Medium and even — keep the root zone consistently moist through spring growth and summer bloom; the martagon is not drought-tolerant and is not a plant for a dry, unwatered xeric bed.[1] At the same time it demands good drainage, especially over winter, when a soggy dormant bulb will rot — moist but never waterlogged is the balance.[1][3]
care.propagation: Slow but rewarding. By seed it shows delayed (hypogeal) germination — a sown seed forms an underground bulblet its first season and only sends up a leaf the next, and seedlings take several years (often 4–7) to reach flowering size; sow ripe seed in a pot in a cold frame and be patient.[2] The faster route is to lift a dormant clump after the foliage dies down and gently separate the offset bulbs or scale off individual bulb-scales to grow on — but because the plant resents disturbance, divide only sparingly.[2][1]
care.bloom: Flowers open mid-to-late summer — roughly July into August on the Front Range — carried as up to about 30 small (occasionally 50 on a strong plant) nodding flowers ranged along the top half of a single stem, each with the tepals fully recurved into the 'Turk's cap', rosy-pink to white and freckled dark maroon inside, and lightly fragrant.[1] A newly planted bulb may not bloom its first year; give it time to establish.[1]
care.feeding: A light feeder. An annual fall top-dressing of leaf mold or compost over the planting area is all it needs; avoid heavy or high-nitrogen fertilizer, which does the bulb no favors.[1]
care.maintenance: Low maintenance once it has settled. Top-dress with compost or leaf mold each fall, protect the emerging spring shoots from slug damage, and cut the stem back to the ground after the leaves yellow.[1] Otherwise leave the plant undisturbed — martagons reward being left alone and resent digging and dividing.[1][2]
care.troubles: Largely free of serious pests and diseases.[1] Watch for the scarlet lily beetle, aphids, thrips, and slugs and snails on the soft new growth, and for rabbits and voles digging the bulbs.[2] In poorly drained or winter-wet soil the bulb is prone to rot, so site it where water never stands.[1]
care.companions: Treat it as a woodland-edge plant and pair it with other part-shade, humus-loving perennials: hostas, ferns, hellebores, brunnera, epimedium, and peonies make good neighbors, and underplanting it beneath deciduous shrubs that cast afternoon shade suits it well. It is at home in shade gardens, mixed and cottage borders, and naturalistic woodland plantings.[1]

edible.summary: The martagon lily is an ornamental, not a food, and like all true lilies (Lilium) it is deadly to cats. Although the bulbs of some Asian lilies are eaten cooked, ornamental martagon bulbs should not be eaten — and no part should ever reach a cat.[2][4]
edible.caution: Two things to know. First, **all true lilies (Lilium species), including the martagon, are extremely toxic to cats**: every part — flowers, leaves, pollen, and even the water a cut stem has stood in — can cause acute kidney failure and death, and there is no safe amount for a cat. Keep the plant and any cut stems well away from cats, and seek emergency veterinary care immediately if a cat ingests any part. (It is non-toxic to dogs and horses.) Second, while the bulbs of a few lilies are cooked and eaten in parts of Asia, the martagon is grown strictly as an ornamental and its bulbs are not a recognized food here; treat the whole plant as not-to-be-eaten, and never confuse ornamental lily bulbs with edible bulbs.[2][4]
